topicArea: Philosophy, Moral Luck & the Limits of Control

questions.passage:
Consider two drivers, otherwise indistinguishable in their habits, who each glance at a text message while driving through a quiet residential street at roughly the same speed. Neither driver is watching the road for the two or three seconds it takes to read the message. In one case, no child happens to be crossing, and the driver arrives home having committed, at most, a minor traffic infraction few would remember by morning. In the other case, a child steps into the street at exactly the wrong moment, and the driver strikes and kills her. Our ordinary moral responses to these two drivers diverge sharply. The first receives a warning, or perhaps a fine, and mild private embarrassment. The second may face criminal charges, lasting public condemnation, and a private burden of guilt that could last the rest of his life. Yet the two drivers behaved identically. Neither controlled whether a child happened to be present. The entire difference in how we judge them traces to a fact, the child's location at a given instant, that neither driver chose or could have chosen. This asymmetry, so familiar that it rarely provokes comment, turns out to be difficult to justify once examined directly.

The difficulty sharpens considerably once a natural principle of moral assessment is made explicit: that people should be judged, morally, only for what lies within their control. Call this the control principle. It has considerable intuitive appeal; it seems to underwrite our reluctance to blame someone for a genetic condition, a natural disaster, or another person's independent choices. But the control principle, applied consistently to the two drivers, yields an uncomfortable conclusion. Since neither driver controlled the child's presence in the street, the control principle implies that they are equally culpable, and that our sharply differing judgments of them, however natural they feel, track nothing but luck. What we call the second driver's greater wrongdoing would then be, strictly, an illusion generated by an outcome that was never within his power to determine.

Most people, confronted with this argument, do not actually abandon their differential judgment, even once they concede its logical force. This resistance deserves attention in its own right rather than dismissal as mere moral confusion. One possible explanation is that outcomes, even when not fully controlled, reveal something real about the stakes of the risk an agent chose to take; texting while driving through a street where children might plausibly be crossing is a gamble with real, foreseeable danger, and the materialization of that danger, though not chosen, is not entirely arbitrary either; it was a live possibility the agent accepted in choosing to look away. A second explanation locates the resistance not in a fact about culpability at all but in the practical functions that blame and punishment serve, functions such as deterrence and compensation to victims, that cannot be neatly separated from actual consequences without abandoning much of what those practices are for.

The driving case is only the most vivid instance of a broader family of problems that philosophers working on this topic have distinguished with some care. Resultant luck, illustrated by the two drivers, concerns luck in how an action turns out. Circumstantial luck concerns luck in the situations an agent happens to face, as when a person who would have behaved corruptly if tempted is never actually tested and so is never revealed, even to himself, as someone capable of corruption. Constitutive luck concerns luck in the temperament and character an agent brings to any situation at all, a temperament shaped by genetic inheritance and upbringing the agent did not select. Once circumstantial and constitutive luck are taken seriously, the original puzzle metastasizes. If a person's disposition toward carefulness or recklessness was itself formed by factors outside her control, then even the trait that supposedly grounds her moral responsibility, an underlying willingness to be careful, turns out on inspection to be one more matter of luck. Pressed to its limit, the control principle threatens not merely to complicate the driving case but to dissolve the very idea of moral responsibility, since no feature of any agent, examined closely enough, seems to survive as genuinely, originally her own.

One response treats this outcome not as a discovery that moral responsibility is illusory but as evidence that the control principle, taken as an exceptionless standard, cannot be correct. Ordinary moral practice, on this view, has never actually operated by isolating some pure, luck-free residue of agency before assigning praise or blame; demanding such a residue may be demanding something that no coherent notion of agency could ever supply, which suggests the flaw lies in the demand rather than in our practices.

A second, narrower response separates the ethics of private moral assessment from the ethics of social and legal response. Even if the two drivers are equally blameworthy in some inner, private sense, institutions of law and social sanction may legitimately track outcomes for reasons that have little to do with comparative inner guilt: deterrence requires visible consequences, victims require some form of acknowledgment or compensation that a near-miss does not generate, and institutions are rarely equipped to assess private culpability directly, only its public traces. This distinction relieves some pressure without resolving the deeper puzzle, since it leaves untouched the fact that the second driver's private guilt, whatever policy may recommend, is likely to feel to him like something more than an artifact of unlucky policy design.

That this puzzle has resisted resolution across a long history of moral philosophy suggests it is not best understood as a problem awaiting a decisive solution. It functions instead as a standing reminder of the distance between the kind of agent morality seems to presuppose, fully self-authored and insulated from luck, and the kind of agent any of us actually is, embedded from the start in circumstances, temperament, and consequences never fully of our own choosing.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Moral responsibility is entirely illusory, since no human action is ever genuinely within an agent's control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The two drivers in the opening example are equally blameworthy in every respect, so any legal or social distinction between them is indefensible.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ordinary judgment tracks outcomes agents did not control, yet restricting assessment to control threatens responsibility itself, a tension left unresolved.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deterrence and compensation to victims are the only legitimate reasons for punishing outcomes rather than intentions.

explanations.A: This choice draws a far stronger conclusion than the passage supports. The author explores serious challenges to the idea that responsibility tracks only what is under an agent's control, but never concludes that moral responsibility is entirely illusory; the fifth paragraph explicitly treats that conclusion as a reason to doubt the control principle rather than to abandon responsibility altogether.
explanations.B: This choice treats one philosophical argument discussed in the passage as though it were the passage's settled conclusion. The control principle does imply that the two drivers are equally culpable, but the passage goes on to complicate this implication, exploring reasons why differential treatment might still be defensible as social policy, rather than declaring the distinction indefensible.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage's central movement is to show that ordinary moral judgment differentiates agents by outcomes they did not control, that a natural principle restricting judgment to what is controlled would deem this indefensible, and that pursuing this principle consistently threatens to dissolve moral responsibility generally, a tension the passage presents as unresolved rather than solved.
explanations.D: This choice isolates one narrow point from the sixth paragraph, which discusses deterrence and compensation as possible policy justifications, and mistakes it for the passage's overall thesis. The passage's central concern is the broader tension between the control principle and ordinary moral practice, not a specific claim about which policy justifications are legitimate.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fourth paragraph, which distinguishes resultant, circumstantial, and constitutive luck, is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: resolve the tension introduced in the second and third paragraphs by offering a precise, philosophically defensible definition of control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: prove that circumstantial luck is a more serious philosophical problem than resultant luck.

choices.label: C
choices.text: argue that moral responsibility should be assigned based on an agent's temperament rather than an agent's specific actions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: show that the driving example's difficulty spreads, threatening moral responsibility generally once control is applied consistently.

explanations.A: This choice misreads the paragraph's function. Rather than resolving the earlier tension by offering a precise definition of control, the fourth paragraph intensifies that tension by showing how difficult a luck-free notion of control is to sustain once circumstantial and constitutive luck are considered.
explanations.B: This choice attributes a comparative ranking to the paragraph that it does not make. The passage does not argue that circumstantial luck is more serious than resultant luck; it treats all three categories as extending the same underlying difficulty rather than ranking them against one another.
explanations.C: This choice mischaracterizes the paragraph's argument as a positive proposal. The paragraph does not argue that responsibility should be assigned based on temperament instead of actions; it argues that even temperament, on close inspection, turns out to be a matter of luck, which is a problem for any account of responsibility, not a solution favoring one basis over another.
explanations.D: Correct. By introducing circumstantial and constitutive luck alongside resultant luck, the paragraph shows that the driving example is only the most vivid instance of a much broader problem, one that ultimately threatens the coherence of moral responsibility itself once the control principle is applied consistently to temperament and disposition as well as to outcomes.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the control principle, taken as an exceptionless standard for moral assessment, is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: fully approving, since the passage endorses the control principle as the correct basis for all legal punishment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: doubtful, treating its consistent application as yielding conclusions responsible moral practice would not accept.

choices.label: C
choices.text: dismissive, since the author regards the control principle as too trivial to merit serious philosophical attention.

choices.label: D
choices.text: approving, but only with respect to circumstantial luck rather than to resultant or constitutive luck, where it plainly fails.

explanations.A: This choice significantly overstates the author's position. The passage never claims the control principle should serve as the basis for all legal punishment; in fact, the sixth paragraph explicitly separates legal and social punishment from questions of inner culpability, suggesting institutions may legitimately track outcomes for reasons unrelated to the control principle.
explanations.B: Correct. The fifth paragraph treats the dissolution of moral responsibility, the conclusion that a consistently applied control principle seems to generate, as evidence that the principle itself must be flawed or in need of qualification, rather than as a discovery to be accepted. This reflects a doubtful attitude toward the principle as an exceptionless standard.
explanations.C: This choice is contradicted by the sustained, careful attention the passage devotes to the control principle across several paragraphs. An author who considered the principle too trivial for serious attention would be unlikely to trace its implications through three distinct categories of luck.
explanations.D: This choice introduces a distinction the passage does not draw. The author's doubts about the control principle, taken as an exceptionless standard, apply to its implications for constitutive and resultant luck as much as for circumstantial luck; nothing in the passage restricts this skepticism to circumstantial cases alone.

questions.stem: The author's use of the word metastasizes in the fourth paragraph most nearly suggests that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the original problem spreads into a much larger challenge to the coherence of moral responsibility.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the driving example described in the first paragraph is a medical case rather than a moral one.

choices.label: C
choices.text: philosophers who study moral luck have reached a stable consensus on how to resolve the underlying puzzle.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the control principle becomes progressively easier to apply once circumstantial and constitutive luck are properly taken into account.

explanations.A: Correct. The verb metastasizes describes how a problem spreads and worsens, and in context it signals that the puzzle first illustrated by the driving example grows, once circumstantial and constitutive luck are introduced, into a far larger challenge to the coherence of moral responsibility as a whole.
explanations.B: This choice confuses a figurative use of a medical term with a literal claim about subject matter. The word metastasizes is used here as a figure of speech describing the spread of a philosophical problem; it does not suggest that the driving example itself is a medical rather than a moral case.
explanations.C: This choice contradicts both the meaning of the word and the passage's overall argument. Metastasizes conveys the intensification of an unresolved difficulty, not the achievement of a stable consensus, and the passage's final paragraph explicitly describes the puzzle as unresolved rather than settled.
explanations.D: This choice reverses the intended meaning. The word describes the control principle's implications becoming more troubling and harder to contain, not easier to apply, once circumstantial and constitutive luck are factored in alongside resultant luck.

questions.stem: According to the passage, each of the following would count as an example of constitutive luck EXCEPT:

choices.label: A
choices.text: A driver whose identical negligence happens to end in a fatal accident while another's does not.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An agent's fundamental temperament, formed by factors entirely outside the agent's control from the earliest stages of development.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The particular willingness to be careful that, on close inspection, appears to be one more matter of luck rather than something the agent originally authored.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A person's underlying disposition toward carefulness or recklessness, shaped by genetic inheritance and upbringing that the person did not select.

explanations.A: Correct. This choice describes resultant luck, not constitutive luck. It concerns two agents who acted identically and differ only in how their actions turned out, matching the passage's definition of resultant luck in its first and fourth paragraphs, which makes it the one option that does not belong among the constitutive-luck examples.
explanations.B: This choice also describes a genuine example of constitutive luck, matching the passage's description of temperament formed by factors outside an agent's control from the earliest stages of development. Because it correctly illustrates constitutive luck, it does not answer the EXCEPT question.
explanations.C: This choice restates the passage's own conclusion about constitutive luck directly: that even a person's specific willingness to be careful turns out, on inspection, to be one more matter of luck. As a correct example of constitutive luck, it is not the exception being sought.
explanations.D: This choice describes a genuine example of constitutive luck as the passage defines it: a disposition toward carefulness or recklessness shaped by genetic inheritance and upbringing the agent did not select. Since this is a correct example of constitutive luck, it is not the exception the question asks for.

questions.stem: Suppose a company disciplines two employees, both equally negligent in failing to review a contract before signing it. One employee's oversight costs the company a minor administrative fee; the other's oversight, due to an unrelated market event neither employee could have anticipated, costs the company several million dollars. The company terminates only the second employee. Based on the passage's discussion of moral luck, this scenario most directly illustrates:

choices.label: A
choices.text: circumstantial luck, since the two employees faced different situations that tested their underlying competence to meaningfully different degrees.

choices.label: B
choices.text: constitutive luck, since the outcome depended on differences in the employees' underlying temperament.

choices.label: C
choices.text: resultant luck, since the employees behaved identically and differ only in an outcome neither controlled.

choices.label: D
choices.text: a case the passage's framework does not address, since it involves a business decision rather than a moral judgment.

explanations.A: This choice misapplies the category of circumstantial luck. Circumstantial luck concerns differences in the situations agents face that test their underlying disposition differently, such as one person being tempted and another never being tested; the two employees in this scenario were not tested differently, they behaved identically and merely experienced different consequences.
explanations.B: This choice misapplies the category of constitutive luck. Constitutive luck concerns differences in an agent's underlying temperament or character, not differences in an action's downstream consequences. The scenario specifies that the employees' underlying conduct, not their temperament, was identical.
explanations.C: Correct. Both employees committed the same negligent oversight, and the outcome, a minor fee in one case and a costly loss in the other, depended entirely on an unrelated market event neither employee controlled. This matches the passage's definition of resultant luck precisely, since the agents' behavior was identical and only the outcome differed.
explanations.D: This choice incorrectly claims the scenario falls outside the passage's framework. The passage's discussion of moral luck applies directly to situations, including business and institutional contexts, in which agents behave identically but are judged differently because of uncontrolled differences in outcome.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the passage's suggestion that our differential moral judgment of the two drivers may be defensible as social policy even if not as a claim about relative inner culpability?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that most people continue to feel greater sympathy for victims of accidents with severe outcomes than for those involved in near-misses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that outcome-based penalties produce no measurable improvement in deterrence or compensation over uniform culpability-based penalties.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that philosophers disagree about the precise boundary between circumstantial and constitutive luck.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that criminal negligence statutes vary significantly in their specific language from one jurisdiction to another.

explanations.A: This choice does not weaken the policy defense; if anything, it is consistent with it. Greater sympathy for victims of severe outcomes could itself be cited as one of the practical considerations, such as the need for acknowledgment or compensation, that the passage suggests might justify differential social or legal response.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage's policy defense depends on outcome-based punishment serving genuine practical purposes such as deterrence and compensation. Evidence that outcome-based penalties do not actually improve these outcomes compared to uniform penalties based on culpable conduct would remove the practical justification the passage offers, directly weakening the claim that the differential treatment is defensible as social policy.
explanations.C: This choice concerns an unrelated disagreement among philosophers about definitional boundaries between types of luck, which does not bear on whether outcome-based punishment achieves the practical goals, deterrence and compensation, that the passage cites as its policy justification.
explanations.D: This choice describes variation in legal language across jurisdictions, which does not address whether outcome-based punishment serves the practical purposes the passage identifies. Variation in statutory wording does not speak to whether such punishment achieves deterrence or compensation any more or less effectively than uniform treatment.
